text: A yellow circle highlights the area of the colon lesion. The laparoscopic dissection starts with the lysis of adhesions from a previous surgery. The surgeon proceeds to dissect across the gastrocolic ligament, around the hepatic flexure, down the right gutter, and finally around the cecum and appendix. Adhesions from the small bowel to the abdominal wall are being carefully taken down using a hook cautery to access the transverse colon.

The surgeon begins the initial dissection to separate the colon from the stomach, lifting the omentum to reveal the colon. The endoscopic tattoo marking the tumor site is visible, and the tumor is palpated with an instrument.

The surgeon opens the gastrocolic ligament, revealing the lesser space behind the stomach. The dissection extends to connect with the previous hepatic flexure dissection. Lifting the omentum exposes the tumor growing through the serosa. Elevating the transverse colon provides a clear view of the hepatic flexure and cecum. Dissection continues around the base of the cecum and appendix, preparing the colon for delivery through a small abdominal incision for resection and anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an incidental cholecystectomy for symptomatic gallstones. The gallbladder is grasped and retracted to expose the base, allowing visualization of the cystic artery and cystic duct, which are then located, clipped, and divided.
The surgeon opens the peritoneal covering at the base and begins dissection superiorly to expose the cystic artery area. The cystic duct is progressively encircled during the procedure.

A small tear in the gallbladder wall is clipped to prevent bile and stone leakage. The cystic duct is encircled, clipped twice on the common duct side, and divided. Dissection continues along the undersurface to encircle the cystic artery, which is then clearly identified, clipped, and divided.
The surgeon frees the gallbladder from its hepatic attachments, amputates it from the liver surface, and secures it with a clamp for removal alongside the colon.

A small upper abdominal incision is made to remove the gallbladder and right colon. The gross specimen is displayed, showing the gallbladder and right colon. A closer view reveals serosal invasion by the tumor, and upon opening the specimen, the primary tumor is visible.
